data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper to buy Dutch coffee giant JDE Peet's for $18B
data.description:
Keurig Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP) will acquire Dutch coffee company JDE Peet's (OTCPK:JDEPF) in an all cash transaction worth about $18.4B (€15.7B), the companies have announced.

The American beverage and coffee maker will pay JDE Peet's (OTCPK:JDEPF) shareholders €31.85 per share in cash, a 33% premium to JDE Peet's 90-day volume-weighted average stock price. The deal will be funded through new senior unsecured and junior subordinated debt and cash on hand.

The acquisition will unlock incremental operating and financial benefits, including around $400M in anticipated cost synergies to be realized over three years and EPS accretion expected to start in the first year of the combination.

Once the acquisition closes, Keurig Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP) plans to split its coffee and beverage units into two separate companies. 

The coffee company, with ~$16B in combined annual net sales, will be the world's largest pure-play coffee company housing $1B+ revenue brands such as Keurig and Jacobs as well as retailer Peet's Coffee.

The beverage company, with over $11B in annual net sales, will house brands such as Dr Pepper, Canada Dry and 7UP.

The combined company will be led by Keurig Dr Pepper's (KDP) management team, including CEO Tim Cofer and CFO Sudhanshu Priyadarshi.

data.title: BlackRock pauses fundraising for latest Asia private credit fund - report
data.description:
BlackRock (NYSE:BLK) has reportedly paused fundraising earlier this year for its latest Asian private credit strategy amid its merger with HPS Investment Partners.

Fundraising for the firm’s third Asia-Pacific private credit fund came to a standstill earlier in 2025 following the announcement in December that BlackRock was buying private credit firm HPS, Bloomberg reported.

The New York-based firm completed its acquisition of HPS on July 1.

data.title: Aegon increases share repurchase plan to €400M
HUTCHMED CEO takes leave of absence; CFO Johnny Cheng appointed acting CEO
Terumo buys OrganOx in $1.5B deal
data.description:
Terumo Corporation (OTCPK:TRUMF) has entered into a definitive agreement to acquire OrganOx Limited, a company specializing in advanced organ preservation devices, for approximately $1.5 billion.

The deal will make OrganOx a wholly owned subsidiary of Terumo once completed.

Founded in 2008 as a spin-off from the University of Oxford, OrganOx is a leader in Normothermic Machine Perfusion technology.

The acquisition combines Terumo's expertise in medical devices with OrganOx's advanced organ preservation technology.

data.title: Starbucks plans to roll back production by two days at US coffee plant to cut costs
data.description:
Starbucks is reducing weekly production by two days in its US roasting and packaging facilities to cut costs.

The coffee chain will adopt a five-day production schedule from seven days at five plants in U.S. starting in January, to cut cost to help pay for upgrades elsewhere. The plants run 24 hours a day.

The five plants, located in Georgia, South Carolina, Pennsylvania, Nevada and Washington state and produce coffee for Starbucks' stores as well as packaged coffee the company sells at retailers and grocery stores.

CEO Niccol, who will complete a year in the job in less than one month, has been cutting cost and reinvesting in its stores in response to weak demand in the United States for its pricey beverages.

Last week, Starbucks capped off a 2% pay raise to all salaried employees. 

Earlier in January, the company had fired corporate staff as part of a turnaround.

data.title: Trump threatens to revoke NBC and ABC licenses over news coverage
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Sunday accused major television networks of biased reporting and suggested that their broadcast licenses should be revoked.

Trump berated NBC and ABC as “two of the worst and most biased networks in history,” adding he would support the FCC in revoking licenses to their television stations.

The networks “give me 97% BAD STORIES,” Trump said in a Truth Social post on Sunday, “despite a very high popularity and, according to many, among the greatest 8 months in Presidential History.”

In a separate post, Trump claimed that the two networks did not pay “Millions of Dollars a year in LICENSE FEES,” and should “at a minimum, pay up BIG for having the privilege of using the most valuable airwaves anywhere at anytime!!!”

Trump has repeatedly criticized news networks for negative coverage and fought high-profile legal battles over it.

Paramount Global, the parent firm of CBS, earlier this year reached an in-principle agreement to pay $16M to settle President Donald Trump's lawsuit over a 60 Minutes interview with former Vice President Kamala Harris.

ABC News last year agreed to settle a defamation lawsuit over inaccurate on-air assertions by anchor George Stephanopoulos.
